label: yes
cot_prefix: Okay, let's tackle this question. The user is asking which conclusion can be drawn from the history experts' statements regarding the "Happy Birthday" song. First, I'll review the passage to find where the experts are mentioned. The relevant part says: "A number of history experts say that there is no record of who actually wrote the 'Happy Birthday to You' lyrics. Historians also say there is no way to know when the general public began singing the 'Happy Birthday' song, but they believe it was being sung by the public long before it was printed and owned by a company." The question is asking what the experts' statements prove. Let's look at the options: A) The song does not belong to Warner Music Group. B) The song has more than 200 years' history. C) Was initially owned by another company. D) Has always been very popular. The experts mention that the song was being sung long before it was printed and owned by a company. That suggests that before Warner Music Group (since 1988) claimed the copyright, the song was in the public domain or common usage. Therefore, it implies that Warner Music Group might not have a valid claim because the song's origin is not attributable to a specific author who could hold the copyright. Option A says the song does not belong to Warner Music Group. The experts don't directly say that Warner doesn't own it, but they do state that there's no record of the original author. In copyright law, if the author can't be determined, the work might be in the public domain. Since the company acquired the copyright in 1988, but the song was in public use long before that, it's possible that the copyright claim is invalid. This would support option A. Option B is about the age of the song. The history of "Good Morning to All" is from 1893, and the lyrics were adapted later. But the passage doesn't say the song has more than 200 years of history. The Hill sisters' version is from 1893, which is less than 130 years ago (since the current year in the text isn't specified, but let's assume it's around the time the article was published, say 2010s). So more than 200 years would be 1820 or earlier. That's not the case here. So B is incorrect. Option C: The experts don't mention another company owning it initially. They just say it was in public use before Warner. So C is incorrect. Option D: The experts don't discuss popularity, just the origin and who owns the copyright. So D is out of scope. Therefore,
